title: Queen Anne style architecture in the United States

infobox.value: was one of a number of popular [Victorian architectural styles](/wiki/Victorian_architectural_styles "Victorian architectural styles") that emerged in the United States during the period from roughly 1880 to 1910.[[1]](#cite_note-1) It is sometimes grouped as [New World Queen Anne Revival architecture](/wiki/New_World_Queen_Anne_Revival_architecture "New World Queen Anne Revival architecture"). Popular there during this time, it followed the [Second Empire](/wiki/Second_Empire_architecture_in_the_United_States_and_Canada "Second Empire architecture in the United States and Canada") and [Stick](/wiki/Stick_style "Stick style") styles and preceded the [Richardsonian Romanesque](/wiki/Richardsonian_Romanesque "Richardsonian Romanesque") and [Shingle](/wiki/Shingle_style_architecture "Shingle style architecture") styles. Sub-movements of Queen Anne include the [Eastlake movement](/wiki/Eastlake_movement "Eastlake movement").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Architectural style during Victorian Era

For the style in other countries, see [Queen Anne Revival architecture](/wiki/Queen_Anne_Revival_architecture_(disambiguation) "Queen Anne Revival architecture (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Frank_Wheeler_Hotel_in_Freetown%2C_Indiana.jpg/330px-Frank_Wheeler_Hotel_in_Freetown%2C_Indiana.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frank_Wheeler_Hotel_in_Freetown,_Indiana.jpg)

Queen Anne style [Frank Wheeler Hotel](/wiki/Frank_Wheeler_Hotel "Frank Wheeler Hotel") in Freetown, Indiana

**Queen Anne style architecture** was one of a number of popular [Victorian architectural styles](/wiki/Victorian_architectural_styles "Victorian architectural styles") that emerged in the United States during the period from roughly 1880 to 1910.[[1]](#cite_note-1) It is sometimes grouped as [New World Queen Anne Revival architecture](/wiki/New_World_Queen_Anne_Revival_architecture "New World Queen Anne Revival architecture"). Popular there during this time, it followed the [Second Empire](/wiki/Second_Empire_architecture_in_the_United_States_and_Canada "Second Empire architecture in the United States and Canada") and [Stick](/wiki/Stick_style "Stick style") styles and preceded the [Richardsonian Romanesque](/wiki/Richardsonian_Romanesque "Richardsonian Romanesque") and [Shingle](/wiki/Shingle_style_architecture "Shingle style architecture") styles. Sub-movements of Queen Anne include the [Eastlake movement](/wiki/Eastlake_movement "Eastlake movement").

The style bears almost no relationship to the original [Queen Anne style architecture](/wiki/Queen_Anne_style_architecture "Queen Anne style architecture") in Britain (a toned-down version of [English Baroque](/wiki/English_Baroque "English Baroque") that was used mostly for [gentry](/wiki/Gentry "Gentry") houses) which appeared during the time of [Queen Anne](/wiki/Anne,_Queen_of_Great_Britain "Anne, Queen of Great Britain"), who reigned from 1702 to 1714, nor of [Queen Anne Revival](/wiki/Queen_Anne_Revival_architecture_in_the_United_Kingdom "Queen Anne Revival architecture in the United Kingdom") (which appeared in the latter 19th century there).

The American style covers a wide range of picturesque buildings with "free Renaissance" (non-[Gothic Revival](/wiki/Gothic_Revival "Gothic Revival")) details, rather than being a specific formulaic style in its own right.

The term "Queen Anne", as an alternative both to the French-derived [Second Empire style](/wiki/Second_Empire_architecture "Second Empire architecture") and the less "domestic" [*Beaux-Arts* style](/wiki/Beaux-Arts_architecture "Beaux-Arts architecture"), is broadly applied to architecture, furniture and decorative arts of the period from 1880 to 1910. Some Queen Anne architectural elements, such as the wrap-around front porch, continued to be found into the 1920s.

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Queen_Anne_style_architecture_in_the_United_States&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/TorringtonGEDC0242_JamesAlldisHouse_sm.JPG/330px-TorringtonGEDC0242_JamesAlldisHouse_sm.JPG)](/wiki/File:TorringtonGEDC0242_JamesAlldisHouse_sm.JPG)

[James Alldis House](/wiki/James_Alldis_House "James Alldis House") in [Torrington, Connecticut](/wiki/Torrington,_Connecticut "Torrington, Connecticut"), built in 1895

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/1800_block_of_California_Street%2C_NW.JPG/330px-1800_block_of_California_Street%2C_NW.JPG)](/wiki/File:1800_block_of_California_Street,_NW.JPG)

Queen Anne style [rowhouses](/wiki/Terraced_house "Terraced house") in the [Adams Morgan](/wiki/Adams_Morgan "Adams Morgan") neighborhood of [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Bembridge_House.jpg/330px-Bembridge_House.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bembridge_House.jpg)

[Bembridge House](/wiki/Bembridge_House "Bembridge House") in [Long Beach, California](/wiki/Long_Beach,_California "Long Beach, California"), built in 1906

Queen Anne style buildings in the United States came into vogue during the 1880s, replacing the French-derived [Second Empire](/wiki/Second_Empire_architecture_in_the_United_States_and_Canada "Second Empire architecture in the United States and Canada") as the 'style of the moment'. The popularity of high Queen Anne style waned in the early 1900s, but some elements continued to be found on buildings into the 1920s, such as the wrap-around front porch (often L-shaped).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Distinctive features of the American Queen Anne style may include:[[2]](#cite_note-2)

* [asymmetrical](/wiki/Asymmetrical "Asymmetrical") façade
* dominant front-facing [gable](/wiki/Gable "Gable"), often [cantilevered](/wiki/Cantilever "Cantilever") beyond the plane of the wall below
* overhanging [eaves](/wiki/Eaves "Eaves")
* round, square, or [polygonal](/wiki/Polygonal "Polygonal") towers
* shaped and [Dutch gables](/wiki/Dutch_gable "Dutch gable")
* a [porch](/wiki/Porch "Porch") covering part or all of the front façade, including the primary entrance area
* a second-story porch or balconies
* [pedimented](/wiki/Pediment "Pediment") porches
* differing wall textures, such as patterned wood shingles shaped into varying designs, including resembling fish scales, [terra cotta](/wiki/Terra_cotta "Terra cotta") tiles, [relief](/wiki/Relief "Relief") panels, or wooden shingles over brickwork, etc.
* [dentils](/wiki/Dentil "Dentil")
* classical columns
* spindle work
* [oriel](/wiki/Oriel_window "Oriel window") and bay windows
* horizontal bands of leaded windows
* monumental chimneys
* painted [balustrades](/wiki/Balustrade "Balustrade")
* usually 2-4 story
* wooden or [slate](/wiki/Slate "Slate") roofs
* front gardens with wooden fences

## Examples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Queen_Anne_style_architecture_in_the_United_States&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Examples")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/120_West_16th_Street_from_west.jpg/330px-120_West_16th_Street_from_west.jpg)](/wiki/File:120_West_16th_Street_from_west.jpg)

The former House and School of Industry at 120 West 16th Street in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/September_1966_DETAIL_CORNER_TOWER_AND_NORTH_%28SIDE%29_ELEVATION_-_Simon_C._Sherwood_House%2C_67_Westway_Road%2C_Southport%2C_Fairfield_County%2C_CT_HABS_CONN%2C1-SOUPO%2C9-2.tif/lossy-page1-250px-thumbnail.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:September_1966_DETAIL_CORNER_TOWER_AND_NORTH_(SIDE)_ELEVATION_-_Simon_C._Sherwood_House,_67_Westway_Road,_Southport,_Fairfield_County,_CT_HABS_CONN,1-SOUPO,9-2.tif)

Simon C. Sherwood House (1884), [Southport, Connecticut](/wiki/Southport,_Connecticut "Southport, Connecticut")

The [British 19th-century Queen Anne style](/wiki/Queen_Anne_style_architecture#British_19th-century_Queen_Anne_style "Queen Anne style architecture") that had been formulated there by [Norman Shaw](/wiki/Norman_Shaw "Norman Shaw") and other architects arrived in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") with the new housing for the New York House and School of Industry[[3]](#cite_note-3) at 120 West 16th Street (designed by [Sidney V. Stratton](/wiki/Sidney_V._Stratton "Sidney V. Stratton"), 1878). The [Astral Apartments](/wiki/Astral_Apartments "Astral Apartments") that were built in [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn") in 1885–1886 (to house workers) are an example of red-brick and terracotta Queen Anne architecture in New York. [E. Francis Baldwin](/wiki/E._Francis_Baldwin "E. Francis Baldwin")'s stations for the [Baltimore and Ohio Railroad](/wiki/Baltimore_and_Ohio_Railroad "Baltimore and Ohio Railroad") are also familiar examples of the style, built variously of brick and wood.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/John_and_Anna_O%27Brien_House%2C_Stillwater%2C_Minnesota_-_DPLA_-_ab51f4a562431d9bb5a46a9f63081ca0_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-John_and_Anna_O%27Brien_House%2C_Stillwater%2C_Minnesota_-_DPLA_-_ab51f4a562431d9bb5a46a9f63081ca0_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_and_Anna_O%27Brien_House,_Stillwater,_Minnesota_-_DPLA_-_ab51f4a562431d9bb5a46a9f63081ca0_(cropped).jpg)

John and Anna O'Brien House in Stillwater, Minnesota, showcasing the prominent gable, pedimented porch and columns, all characteristic of the Queen Anne style

Gabled and domestically scaled, these early American Queen Anne homes were built of warm, soft brick enclosing square [terracotta](/wiki/Terracotta "Terracotta") panels, with an arched side passage leading to an inner court and back house. Their detailing is largely confined to the treatment of picturesquely disposed windows, with small-paned upper sashes and plate glass lower ones. Triple windows of a Serlian motif and a two-story [oriel window](/wiki/Oriel_window "Oriel window") that projects asymmetrically were frequently featured.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The most famous American Queen Anne residence is the [Carson Mansion](/wiki/Carson_Mansion "Carson Mansion") in [Eureka, California](/wiki/Eureka,_California "Eureka, California").[[5]](#cite_note-LOC-5) Newsom and Newsom were notable builder-architects of 19th-century California homes and public buildings, and they designed and constructed (1884–1886) this 18-room home for William Carson, one of California's first [lumber barons](/wiki/Lumber_baron "Lumber baron").

### Free Classic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Queen_Anne_style_architecture_in_the_United_States&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Free Classic")]

After 1885, use of [Eastlake](/wiki/Eastlake_architecture "Eastlake architecture")-style trim shifted to "free classic" or [Colonial Revival](/wiki/Colonial_Revival_architecture "Colonial Revival architecture") trim, including [pedimented](/wiki/Pedimented "Pedimented") entryways and [Palladian windows](/wiki/Palladian_windows "Palladian windows").[[6]](#cite_note-6)

### Queen Anne cottage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Queen_Anne_style_architecture_in_the_United_States&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Queen Anne cottage")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/William_G._Harrison_House%2C_Nashville%2C_GA%2C_USA_%2802%29.jpg/330px-William_G._Harrison_House%2C_Nashville%2C_GA%2C_USA_%2802%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:William_G._Harrison_House,_Nashville,_GA,_USA_(02).jpg)

[William G. Harrison House](/wiki/William_G._Harrison_House "William G. Harrison House"), a Queen Anne cottage

Smaller and somewhat plainer houses can also be Queen Anne. The [William G. Harrison House](/wiki/William_G._Harrison_House "William G. Harrison House") is an example, built in 1904 in rural [Nashville, Georgia](/wiki/Nashville,_Georgia "Nashville, Georgia"). Characteristics of the Queen Anne cottage style are:

* one- or two-story frame house (second floor where one exists, is a finished [attic](/wiki/Attic "Attic"))
* wrap-around porch with turned posts, decorative brackets, and spindle work
* square layout with projecting gables to front and side
* pyramidal or hipped roof reflecting pyramidal massing
* rooms are asymmetrical and there is no central hallway
* interior-located chimneys
* interior detailing, such as door surrounds, window surrounds, [wainscoting](/wiki/Wainscoting "Wainscoting"), and [mantels](/wiki/Mantels "Mantels")
* built in 1880s and 1890s for middle class in both urban and rural areas, with popularity in rural areas continuing into early 1900s.[[7]](#cite_note-nrhpdoc-Harrison-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

### Shingle style

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Queen_Anne_style_architecture_in_the_United_States&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Shingle style")]

Main article: [Shingle style architecture](/wiki/Shingle_style_architecture "Shingle style architecture")

"Shingle house" redirects here. For the community in the United States, see [Shinglehouse, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Shinglehouse,_Pennsylvania "Shinglehouse, Pennsylvania").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Kragsyde%2C_Manchester%2C_MA.jpg/250px-Kragsyde%2C_Manchester%2C_MA.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kragsyde,_Manchester,_MA.jpg)

[Kragsyde](/wiki/Kragsyde "Kragsyde"), Manchester-by-the-Sea, Massachusetts (1883, demolished 1929), by [Peabody and Stearns](/wiki/Peabody_and_Stearns "Peabody and Stearns")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/William_Berryman_Scott_House.jpg/330px-William_Berryman_Scott_House.jpg)](/wiki/File:William_Berryman_Scott_House.jpg)

The [William Berryman Scott](/wiki/William_Berryman_Scott "William Berryman Scott") House (1888), designed by [A. Page Brown](/wiki/A._Page_Brown "A. Page Brown"), at 56 Bayard Lane, [Princeton, New Jersey](/wiki/Princeton,_New_Jersey "Princeton, New Jersey"), in the [Princeton Historic District](/wiki/Princeton_Historic_District_(Princeton,_New_Jersey) "Princeton Historic District (Princeton, New Jersey)")

The Shingle style in America was made popular by the rise of the New England school of architecture, which eschewed the highly ornamented patterns of the [Eastlake style](/wiki/Eastlake_Movement "Eastlake Movement"). In the Shingle style, English influence was combined with the renewed interest in [Colonial American architecture](/wiki/American_colonial_architecture "American colonial architecture") which followed the 1876 celebration of the United States Centennial. Architects emulated colonial houses' plain, shingled surfaces as well as their massing, whether in the simple gable of [McKim, Mead and White](/wiki/McKim,_Mead_%26_White "McKim, Mead & White")'s [Low House](/wiki/William_D._Low_House "William D. Low House") or in the complex massing of [Kragsyde](/wiki/Kragsyde "Kragsyde"), which looked almost as if a colonial house had been fancifully expanded over many years. This impression of the passage of time was enhanced by the use of shingles. Some architects, in order to attain a weathered look on a new building, even had the cedar shakes dipped in buttermilk, dried, and then installed, to leave a grayish tinge to the façade.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The shingle-style also conveyed a sense of the house as continuous volume. This effect—of the building as an envelope of space, rather than a great mass, was enhanced by the visual tautness of the flat shingled surfaces, the horizontal shape of many shingle-style houses, and the emphasis on horizontal continuity, both in exterior details and in the flow of spaces within the houses.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

McKim, Mead and White and [Peabody and Stearns](/wiki/Peabody_and_Stearns "Peabody and Stearns") were two of the notable firms of the era that helped to popularize the shingle style, through their large-scale commissions for "seaside cottages" of the rich and the well-to-do in such places as Newport, Rhode Island. However, the most famous Shingle-style house built in America was "Kragsyde" (1882), the summer home commissioned by Bostonian G. Nixon Black, from Peabody and Stearns. Kragsyde was built atop the rocky coastal shore near [Manchester-by-the-Sea, Massachusetts](/wiki/Manchester-by-the-Sea,_Massachusetts "Manchester-by-the-Sea, Massachusetts"), and embodied every possible tenet of the shingle style.

Many of the concepts of the Shingle style were adopted by [Gustav Stickley](/wiki/Gustav_Stickley "Gustav Stickley"), and adapted to the American version of the [Arts and Crafts Movement](/wiki/Arts_and_Crafts_Movement "Arts and Crafts Movement").

## References

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Queen_Anne_style_architecture_in_the_United_States&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: References")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-1)** McAlester, Virginia & Lee, *A Field Guide to American Houses*, Alfred H. Knopf, New York, 1984, p. 262–287.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-2)** ["Queen Anne Style"](https://buffaloah.com/a/archsty/queen/index.html). *buffaloah.com*.
